title: Gastrointestinal Health. Influence of Grape Consumption on the Human Microbiome. This study analyzed the microbiome composition as well as urinary and plasma metabolites in human subjects that had consumed 2 1/4 cups of grapes per day for two weeks within an eight-week study. Following two weeks of grape consumption, taxonomic abundance was altered with changes observed in the amounts of specific bacteria detected and in enzyme levels and biological pathways. These types of changes were seen again 30 days following the end of grape consumption, some of which returned to baseline levels and others suggesting a prolonged effect of grape consumption. Analysis of a subgroup of subjects showed unique patterns of microbe distribution. While grape consumption did not change the eubiotic state of the microbiome, it does appear to trigger shifts in the intricate interactive networks that may have physiological influence.

sentences.text: Gastrointestinal Health.
Influence of Grape Consumption on the Human Microbiome.
This study analyzed the microbiome composition as well as urinary and plasma metabolites in human subjects that had consumed 2 1/4 cups of grapes per day for two weeks within an eight-week study.
Following two weeks of grape consumption, taxonomic abundance was altered with changes observed in the amounts of specific bacteria detected and in enzyme levels and biological pathways.
These types of changes were seen again 30 days following the end of grape consumption, some of which returned to baseline levels and others suggesting a prolonged effect of grape consumption.
Analysis of a subgroup of subjects showed unique patterns of microbe distribution.
While grape consumption did not change the eubiotic state of the microbiome, it does appear to trigger shifts in the intricate interactive networks that may have physiological influence.
Effect of Standardized Grape Powder Consumption on the Gut Microbiome of Healthy Subjects: A Pilot Study.
Human subjects consuming the equivalent of 1.5 cups of grapes per day for four weeks showed increased microbial diversity as measured by the Shannon Index.
Among the beneficial bacteria that increased was Akkermansia, which has been linked to beneficial effects on glucose and lipid metabolism and on the integrity of the intestinal lining.

Grape consumption significantly modified cholesterol and bile metabolism as well, decreasing total cholesterol by 6.1 percent and H D L cholesterol by 7.6 percent, with a trend of decreasing L D L cholesterol by 5.9 percent.
Bile acids were significantly reduced by 40.9 percent.
Results of a Phase One Clinical Trial Examining the Effect of Plant-Derived Resveratrol and Grape Powder on Wnt Pathway Target Gene Expression in Colonic Mucosa and Colon Cancer.
In this study, normal colonic mucosa and colon cancer tissue were examined pre- and post-exposure to grape powder by oral consumption.
The subjects consumed whole grape powder every day for two weeks; samples of healthy and cancerous colon tissues were taken before and after consuming the grape diet.

The results showed that subjects consuming the equivalent of 2 1/2 cups of grapes for two weeks showed a 47 percent reduction in the expression of certain target genes involved in the W N T-signaling pathway that are responsible for promoting tumor growth in the colon.
This beneficial effect was seen in the subjects' healthy colon tissue, but not the cancerous, suggesting that regular consumption of grapes may help to maintain a healthy colon.
Effect of a Grape-Supplemented Diet on Proliferation and Wnt Signaling in the Colonic Mucosa Are Greatest for Those Over Age 50 and with High Arginine Consumption.
In this study, 30 healthy subjects were randomly assigned to one of three diets: consuming either one-third, two-thirds, or one pound of grapes each day for two weeks.
Samples of colon tissue were taken before and after consuming the grape diets.
The results showed that grape consumption at all three levels inhibited the expression of Wnt pathway target genes and markers of mucosal proliferation involved in cancer promotion.

The researchers noted that the potential benefits of grape intake are most significant for high-risk older individuals and individuals whose diet is high in arginine intake; both of these groups have an increased incidence of colon cancer.
This study reinforces previous findings that suggest a potential protective role for grapes against colon cancer.
Multi-organ Nutrigenomic Effects of Dietary Grapes in a Mouse Model.

In a year-long study where mice consumed a grape-enriched diet equivalent to the human consumption of 2.5 servings of grapes per day, study findings showed the ability of grapes to modulate gene expression in the colon.

By enriching and up-regulating beneficial pathways while down-regulating harmful pathways, researchers suggested that the grape diet promoted anti-inflammatory activity, suppressed mitochondrial dysfunction, maintained cell balance, homeostasis, and inhibited cancer-promoting pathways.
Grape Consumption Reduces Adiposity and Markers of Hepatic Lipogenesis and Alters Gut Microbiota in Butter Fat-Fed Mice.

In this study, consuming either 3 or 5 percent grapes in addition to a high-fat diet for 11 weeks reduced the percentage of body fat, including subcutaneous and visceral fat deposits, reduced markers of inflammation, and improved glucose tolerance and intestinal barrier function.
Those consuming 3 percent grapes exhibited an increase in some beneficial gut bacteria, Akkermansia muciniphila, and a decrease in detrimental bacteria.
Further, the grape-enriched diet mitigated high-fat-induced impairment to the intestine, commonly known as leaky gut.
Grape-fed mice at either the 3 or 5 percent diet showed reduced total body and inguinal fat and displayed lower liver weights and triglyceride levels.
The grape diet had a minor effect on markers of inflammation or lipogenesis in adipose tissue or intestine.
